In [[Europe]], the leading prey species numerically (the main prey species in 41% of 32 European studies largely focused on the nesting season) is the {{convert|352|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[rock pigeon]] (''Columba livia'').<ref name= Kenward/> Although the predominance of [[Feral pigeon|rock pigeons in urban environments]] that host goshawks such as the [[Germany|German]] cities of [[Hamburg]] (where they constituted 36% by number and nearly 45% by weight of the local diet) or [[Cologne]] is predictable, evidence shows that these development-clinging pigeons are sought out even within ample conserved woodland from [[Portugal]] to [[Georgia (country)|Georgia]].<ref name= Rutz2/><ref name= Skilsky>{{cite journal |last1= Skilsky |first1= I. V. |last2= Meleshchuk |first2= L. I. |year=2007 |title= Diet of the Goshawk in the southern part of the Ukraine |journal= Berkut |volume= 16 |issue= 1|pages= 159–161 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Petronilho |first1= J. |last2= Vingada |first2= J. V. |year=2002 |title= ''First data on feeding ecology of Goshawk Accipiter gentilis during the breeding season in the natura 2000 site Dunas de Mira, Gândara e Gafanhas (Beira Litoral, Portugal)'' |journal= Airo |volume= 12 |pages= 11–16 }}</ref><ref name= Abuladze>Abuladze, A. (2013). ''Birds of Prey of Georgia''. Materials towards Fauna of Georgia, Issue VI, Institute of Zoology, Ilia State University.</ref> In areas where goshawk restrict their hunting forays to field and forest, they often catch another numerous pigeon, the {{convert|490|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[common wood pigeon]] (''Columba palumbus'') (the largest pigeon the goshawk naturally encounters and is known to hunt). The latter species was the main prey in the diet of goshawks in the [[Germany-Netherlands border]] area (37.7% of 4125 prey items) and [[Wales]] (25.1% by number and 30.5% by biomass of total prey).<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Opdam>Opdam, P. F. M. (1980). ''Feeding ecology and niche differentiation in goshawk (''Accipiter gentilis'' L.) and sparrowhawk (''Accipiter nisus'' L.)'' (Doctoral dissertation, [Sl]: PFM Opdam).</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Toyne |first1= E. P. |year=1998 |title= Breeding season diet of the Goshawk Accipiter gentilis in Wales |doi= 10.1111/j.1474-919x.1998.tb04701.x |journal= Ibis |volume= 140 |issue= 4|pages= 569–579 }}</ref> It has been theorized that male goshawks in peri-urban regions may be better suited with their higher agility to ambushing feral pigeons in and amongst various manmade structures whereas females may be better suited due to the higher overall speeds to taking out common wood-pigeons, as these typically forage in wood-cloaked but relatively open fields; however males are efficient predators of common wood-pigeons as well.<ref name= Kenward/><ref>Brüll, H. (1964). ''Das Leben deutscher Greifvögel''. Fischer, Sttugart, Germany.</ref> Studies have proven that, while hunting feral pigeons, goshawks quite often select the oddly colored pigeons out of flocks as prey, whether the plumage of the flock is predominantly dark or light hued, they disproportionately often select individuals of the other color. This preference is apparently more pronounced in older, experienced goshawks and there is some evidence that the males who select oddly-colored pigeons have higher average productivity during breeding.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Pielowski |first1= Z |year=1961 |title= Uber den Unifikationseinfluss der selektiven Narhungswahl des Habichts, Accipiter gentilis L., auf Haustauben |journal= Ekologa Polska |volume= 9 |pages= 183–194 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Rutz |first1= C |year=2012 |title= Predator fitness increases with selectivity for odd prey |journal= Current Biology |volume= 22 |issue= 9|pages= 820–824 |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2012.03.028|pmid= 22503502 |doi-access= free |bibcode= 2012CBio...22..820R }}</ref>

===Gamebirds===
[[File:Hawk and Black-Game (Bruno Liljefors) - Nationalmuseum - 19423.tif|thumb|left|''Hawk and Black-Game'' ([[Bruno Liljefors]], 1884), a painting of a goshawk at the moment of catching a [[black grouse]]]]
The Eurasian goshawk is in some parts of its range considered a specialized predator of [[Galliformes|gamebirds]], particularly [[grouse]]. All told 33 species of this order have turned up in Eurasian and [[American goshawk]]s' diets, including most of the species either native to or introduced in North America and Europe. Numerically, only in the well-studied [[taiga]] habitats of [[Scandinavia]] do grouse typically take a dominant position. Elsewhere in the range, gamebirds are often secondary in number but often remain one of the most important contributors of prey biomass to nests. With their general ground-dwelling habits, gamebirds tend to be fairly easy for goshawks to overtake if they remain unseen and, if made aware of the goshawk, the prey chooses to run rather than fly. If frightened too soon, gamebirds may take flight and may be chased for some time, although the capture rates are reduced considerably when this occurs. Pre-fledgling chicks of gamebirds are particularly vulnerable due to the fact that they can only run when being pursued.<ref name="Brown"/><ref name= Kenward/> In several parts of [[Scandinavia]], forest grouse have historically been important prey for goshawks both in and out of the nesting season, principally the {{convert|1080|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[black grouse]] (''Lyrurus tetrix'') and the {{convert|430|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[hazel grouse]] (''Tetrastes bonasia'') followed in numbers by larger {{convert|2950|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[western capercaillie]]s (''Tetrao urogallus'') and the {{convert|570|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[willow ptarmigan]] (''Lagopus lagopus'') which replace the other species in the lower tundra zone. The impression of goshawks on the populations of this prey is considerable, possibly the most impactful of any predator in northern Europe considering their proficiency as predators and similarity of habitat selection to forest grouse. An estimated 25-26% of adult hazel grouses in Finnish and Swedish populations in a few studies fall victim to goshawks, whereas about 14% of adult black grouse are lost to this predator. Lesser numbers were reportedly culled in one study from northern [[Finland]]. However, adult grouse are less important in the breeding season diet than young birds, an estimated 30% of grouse taken by Scandinavian goshawks in summer were neonatal chicks whereas 53% were about fledgling age, the remaining 17% being adult grouse.<ref name= Kenward/><ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Widen>{{cite journal | last1 = Widen | first1 = P | year = 1987 | title = Goshawk predation during winter, spring and summer in a boreal forest area in Sweden | journal = Ornis Fennica | volume = 61 | pages = 109–112 }}</ref><ref>Linden, H., & Wikman, M. (1983). ''Goshawk predation on tetraonids: availability of prey and diet of the predator in the breeding season''. ''The Journal of Animal Ecology'', 953-968.</ref><ref name= Tornberg>{{cite journal | last1 = Tornberg | first1 = R. | last2 = Korpimaki | first2 = E. | last3 = Byholm | first3 = P. | year = 2006 | title = Ecology of the northern goshawk in Fennoscandia | journal = Studies in Avian Biology | volume = 31 | pages = 141–157 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Tornberg |first1= R |year=2001 |title= Pattern of goshawk Accipiter gentilis predation on four forest grouse species in northern Finland |journal= Wildlife Biology |volume= 7 |issue= 4|pages= 245–256 |doi= 10.2981/wlb.2001.029 |s2cid= 90797670 |doi-access= free |bibcode= 2001WildB...7..245T }}</ref> This is fairly different from in southeastern [[Alaska]], where grouse are similarly as important as in Fennoscandia, as 32.1% of avian prey deliveries were adults, 14.4% were fledglings and 53.5% were nestlings.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Lewis |first1= S. B. |last2= Fuller |first2= M. R. |last3= Titus |first3= K. |year=2004 |title= A comparison of 3 methods for assessing raptor diet during the breeding season |journal= Wildlife Society Bulletin |volume= 32 |issue= 2|pages= 373–385 |doi=10.2193/0091-7648(2004)32[373:acomfa]2.0.co;2|s2cid= 84991855 }}</ref>

[[File:Accipiter gentilis f juv captive.jpg|thumb|right|Goshawks sometimes become habitual fowl killers. This juvenile was caught pursuing chickens inside a hen house.]]
Eurasian goshawks can show somewhat of a trend for females to be taken more so than males while hunting adult gamebirds, due to the larger size and more developed defenses of males (such as leg spurs present for defense and interspecies conflicts in male of most pheasant species). Some authors have claimed this of male [[ring-necked pheasant]] (''Phasianus colchicus''), but these trends are not reported everywhere, as in southern [[Sweden]] equal numbers of adult male and female ring-necked pheasants, both sexes averaging {{convert|1135|g|lb|abbr=on}}, were taken.<ref name= Kenward/><ref name= "CRC"/><ref>Marcstrom, V. & Widen, P. (1977). ''Hur skulle det ga for duvhoken om inte Fasan fanns?'' Svensk Jakt, 115: 98–101.</ref> While male goshawks can take black and hazel grouse of any age and thence deliver them to nests, they can only take capercaillie of up to adult hen size, averaging some {{convert|1800|g|lb|abbr=on}}, the cock capercaillie at more than twice as heavy as the hen is too large for a male goshawk to overtake. However, adult female goshawks have been reported attacking and killing cock capercaillie, mainly during winter. These average about {{convert|4000|g|lb|abbr=on}} in body mass and occasionally may weigh even more when dispatched.<ref name= Kenward/><ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= profitability/><ref name= Widen/><ref name= Tornberg/> Similarly impressive feats of attacks on other particularly large gamebirds have been reported elsewhere in the range, including the {{convert|2770|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[Altai snowcock]] (''Tetraogallus altaicus'') in [[Mongolia]]<ref name= "CRC"/><ref>Badarch, D., Zilinskas, R. A., & Balint, P. J. (2003). ''Mongolia today: science, culture, environment and development (Vol. 1)''. Psychology Press.</ref> At the other end of the size scale, the smallest gamebird known to be hunted by Eurasian goshawk was the {{convert|96|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[common quail]] (''Coturnix coturnix'').<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name="Mañosa">{{cite journal |last1= Mañosa |first1= S |year=1994 |title= Goshawk diet in a Mediterranean area of northeastern Spain |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 28 |issue= 2|pages= 84–92 }}</ref> Domestic fowl, particularly [[chicken]]s (''Gallus gallus domesticus'') are taken occasionally, especially where wild prey populations are depleted. While other raptors are at times blamed for large numbers of attacks on fowl, goshawks are reportedly rather more likely to attack chickens during the day than other raptors and are probably the most habitual avian predator of domestic fowl, at least in the temperate-zone. Particularly large numbers of chickens have been reported in [[Wigry National Park]], [[Poland]] (4th most regular prey species and contributing 15.3% of prey weight), [[Belarus]] and the [[Ukraine]], being the third most regularly reported prey in the latter two.<ref name=Rashid/><ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name= Slisky>{{cite journal | last1 = Slisky | first1 = I. V. | last2 = Meleshchuk | first2 = L. I. | year = 2007 | title = Diet of the goshawk in southern part of western Ukraine | journal = Berkut | volume = 16 | issue = 1| pages = 159–161 }}</ref><ref name= Ivanovsky>Ivanovsky, V. V. (1998). ''Current status and breeding ecology of the Goshawk Accipiter gentilis in northern Belarus''. Holarctic Birds of Prey, ADENEXWWGBP, Calamonte, Spain, 111–115.</ref>

In a study of British goshawks, the [[red grouse]] (''Lagopus scotica'') was found to be the leading prey species (26.2% of prey by number).<ref name= Marquiss>{{cite journal | last1 = Marquiss | first1 = M. | last2 = Newton | first2 = I. | year = 1982 | title = The goshawk in Britain | journal = British Birds | volume = 75 | pages = 243–260 }}</ref> In [[Segarra|La Segarra]], [[Spain]], the {{convert|528|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[red-legged partridge]] (''Alectoris rufa'') is the most commonly reported prey species (just over 18% by number and 24.5% by weight).<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name="Mañosa"/>

===Squirrels===

Among mammalian prey, indisputably the most significant by number are the squirrels. All told, 44 members of the [[Sciuridae]] have turned up in their foods.{{efn|This includes Sciuridae species taken by the American goshawk, now treated as a separate species.}} Tree squirrels are the most obviously co-habitants with goshawks and are indeed taken in high numbers. Alongside [[marten]]s, Eurasian goshawks are perhaps the most efficient temperate-zone predators of tree squirrels. Goshawks are large and powerful enough to overtake even the heaviest tree squirrels unlike smaller ''Accipiters'' and have greater agility and endurance in pursuits than do most buteonine hawks, some of which like [[red-tailed hawk]]s (''Buteo jamaicensis'') regularly pursue tree squirrels but have relatively low hunting success rates due to the agility of squirrels.<ref name= Kenward/><ref>McComb, A. M. (2004). ''Raptor Predation Attempts on Grey Squirrels Sciurus carolinensis (Gmelin)''. The Irish Naturalists' Journal, 27(12), 483–484.</ref> The {{convert|296|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[red squirrel]] (''Sciurus vulgaris'') is the most numerous mammalian prey in European studies and the sixth most often recorded prey species there overall.<ref name= Kenward/><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Wauters |first1= L. A. |last2= Dhondt |first2= A. A. |year=1989 |title= Variation in length and body weight of the red squirrel (Sciurus vulgaris) in two different habitats |journal= Journal of Zoology |volume= 217 |issue= 1|pages= 93–106 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.1989.tb02477.x |bibcode= 1989JZoo..217...93W }}</ref> In [[Oulu]], [[Finland]] during winter (24.6% by number), in [[Białowieża Forest]], [[Poland]] (14.3%), in the [[Chřiby|Chřiby uplands]] of the [[Czech Republic]] (8.5%) and in [[List of forests in France|Forêt de Bercé]], [[France]] (12%) the red squirrel was the main prey species for goshawks.<ref name="Białowieża">{{cite journal |last1= Gompper |first1= M. E. |year=1999 |title= Predation in Vertebrate Communities: The Białowieża Primeval Forest as a Case Study |jstor= 176929 |journal= Ecology |volume= 80 |issue= 7|pages= 2450–1 |doi= 10.1890/0012-9658(1999)080[2450:PPDAAE]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Tomešek |first1= M. |last2= Čermák |first2= P. |year=2014 |title= Distribution and biology of goshawk (Accipiter gentilis L.) in the Chřiby Upland, the Czech Republic |journal= Acta Universitatis Agriculturae et Silviculturae Mendelianae Brunensis |volume= 57 |issue= 1|pages= 153–164 |doi= 10.11118/actaun200957010153 |doi-access= free }}</ref><ref>Vaidie, F. (1999). ''L'Autour des palombes (Accipiter gentilis) en Sarthe''.</ref>

===Hares and rabbits===
[[File:The Rabbit (1898) 'Just missed him'.png|thumb|Illustrating a goshawk attempting to catch a [[European rabbit|rabbit]], by [[George Edward Lodge|G. E. Lodge]]]]
Eurasian goshawks can be locally heavy predators of [[lagomorph]]s, of which they take at least 15 species as prey. Especially in the [[Iberian peninsula]], the native [[European rabbit]] (''Oryctolagus cuniculus'') is often delivered to nests and can be the most numerous prey. Even where taken secondarily in numbers in Spain to gamebirds such as in [[Segarra|La Segarra]], [[Spain]], rabbits tend to be the most significant contributor of biomass to goshawk nests. On average, the weight of rabbits taken in La Segarra was {{convert|662|g|lb|abbr=on}} (making up 38.4% of the prey biomass there), indicating most of the 333 rabbits taken there were yearlings and about 2-3 times lighter than a prime adult wild rabbit.<ref name="Mañosa"/><ref name= Zuberogoitia/> In [[England]], where the European rabbit is an introduced species, it was the third most numerous prey species at nests.<ref name= Marquiss/> In more snowbound areas where wild and feral rabbits are absent, larger [[hare]]s may be taken and while perhaps more difficult to subdue than most typical goshawk prey, are a highly nutritious food source. In [[Finland]], females were found to take [[mountain hare]] (''Lepus timidus'') fairly often and they were the second most numerous prey item for goshawks in winter (14.8% by number).<ref name= Tornberg/> In some parts of the range, larger leporids may be attacked, extending to the {{convert|3800|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[European hare]]s (''Lepus europaeus''), as well as the mountain hare.<ref name=Drennan>{{cite journal |last1= Drennan |first1= J. E. |year=2006 |title= Northern goshawk food habits and goshawk prey species habitats |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 31 |pages= 198–227 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Pfeiffer | first1 = W | year = 1978 | title = On the capture of a hare (Lepus europaeus) by a Northern Goshawk (Accipiter gentilis) | journal = Aves | volume = 15 | pages = 31–33 }}</ref><ref>Chapman, J. A., & Flux, J. E. (1990). ''Rabbits, hares and pikas: status survey and conservation action plan''. IUCN.</ref> In Europe, males have been recorded successfully attacking rabbits weighing up to {{convert|1600|g|lb|abbr=on}}, or about 2.2 times their own weight, while adult mountain hares overtaken by female goshawks in Fennoscandia have weighed from {{convert|2700|to|3627|g|lb|abbr=on}} or up to 2.4 times their own weight. Despite historic claims that taking prey so considerably larger than themselves is exceptional beyond a small region of [[Fennoscandia]], there is evidence that as grouse numbers have mysteriously declined since 1960, adult mountain hare are increasingly the leading prey for wintering female goshawks, favouring and causing an increase of larger bodied females in order to overpower such a substantial catch.<ref name= Kenward/><ref name= Kenward2>{{cite journal |last1= Kenward |first1= R. E. |last2= Marcstrom |first2= V. |last3= Karlbom |first3= M. |year=1981 |title= Goshawk winter ecology in Swedish pheasant habitats |journal= J. Wildl. Manage. |volume= 45 |issue= 2|pages= 397–408 |doi=10.2307/3807921|jstor= 3807921 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Tornberg |first1= R. |last2= Mönkkönen |first2= M. |last3= Pahkala |first3= M. |year=1999 |title= Changes in diet and morphology of Finnish goshawks from 1960s to 1990s |journal= Oecologia |volume= 121 |issue= 3|pages= 369–376 |doi=10.1007/s004420050941|pmid= 28308326 |bibcode= 1999Oecol.121..369T |s2cid= 3217469 }}</ref> Eurasian goshawks also take about a half dozen species of [[pika]]s in Asia, much smaller cousins of rabbits and hares, but they are at best supplementary prey for [[American goshawk]]s and of unknown importance to little-studied Eurasian goshawks.<ref>Bleisch, W. (2011). ''Asian Highlands Perspectives 18: Environmental Issues Facing Tibetan Pastoral Communities (Vol. 31)''. ASIAN HIGHLANDS PERSPECTIVES.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Smith |first1= A. T. |last2= Foggin |first2= J. M. |year=1999 |title= The plateau pika (Ochotona curzoniae) is a keystone species for biodiversity on the Tibetan plateau |journal= Animal Conservation |volume= 2 |issue= 4|pages= 235–240 |doi=10.1017/s1367943099000566}}</ref>

===Other birds===
Some 21 species of [[woodpecker]] have been reported from Eurasian and American goshawk food studies around the world. With their relatively slow, undulating flight, adult and fledged woodpeckers can easily be overtaken by hunting goshawks, not to mention their habitat preferences frequently put them within active goshawk ranges. Most of the widespread species from Europe have been observed as prey, most commonly relatively large woodpeckers such as the {{convert|76|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[great spotted woodpecker]] (''Dendrocopos major'') and the {{convert|176|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[European green woodpecker]] (''Picus viridis'').<ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name= Schreven>Schreven, K. ''Een luchtbukskogeltje in een braakbal van een Havik Accipiter gentilis, in de context van jacht en loodvergifti-ging''. De Takkeling, 225–229.</ref> All sizes of woodpeckers available are taken from the {{convert|19.8|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[lesser spotted woodpecker]] (''Dryobates minor'') to the {{convert|321|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[black woodpecker]] (''Dryocopus martius'').<ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name= Schaffer>Schaffer, W. W. 1998. ''Northern goshawk (Accipiter gentilis) habitat characterization in central Alberta''. Thesis, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta.</ref> In many areas, Eurasian goshawks will pursue [[water bird]]s of several varieties, although they rarely form a large portion of the diet. Perhaps the most often recorded water birds in the diet are [[duck]]s. All told, 32 waterfowl have been recorded in Eurasian and American goshawks' diet.<ref name= Kenward/> In the [[Ural Mountain]]s, the nearly cosmopolitan {{convert|1075|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[mallard]] (''Anas platyrhynchos'') was third most numerous prey species.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Karyakin/> The ducks of the genus ''[[Aythya]]'' are somewhat frequently recorded as well, especially since their tree-nesting habits may frequently put them in the hunting range of nesting goshawks.<ref name=Smithers2005>{{cite journal |last1=Smithers |first1=B. L. |last2=Boal |first2=C. W. |last3=Andersen |first3=D. E. |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v039n03/p00264-p00273.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk diet in Minnesota: An analysis using video recording systems |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |year=2005 |volume=39 |issue=3 |pages=264–273 |bibcode=2005JRapR..39..264S }}</ref><ref name=Lewis2006>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Stephen B. |last2=Titus |first2=Kimberly |last3=Fuller |first3=Mark R. |url=http://fresc.usgs.gov/products%5Cpapers%5C1547_Fuller.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk Diet During the Nesting Season in Southeast Alaska |doi=10.2193/0022-541X(2006)70[1151:NGDDTN]2.0.CO;2 |year=2006 |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=70 |issue=4 |pages=1151–1160|s2cid=59493648 }}</ref> Similarly, the [[mandarin duck]] (''Aix galericulata'') from Asia may be more vulnerable than most waterfowl at their tree nests.<ref>Lever, C. (2013). ''The mandarin duck''. Bloomsbury Publishing.</ref> Although etymologists feel that the goshawk is an abbreviation of "goose-hawk", geese are seldom taken considering their generally much larger size. Nonetheless, four species have been taken, including adults of species as large as the {{convert|2420|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[greater white-fronted goose]] (''Anser albifrons'').<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Veldkamp>{{cite journal | last1 = Veldkamp | first1 = R | year = 2008 | title = Cormorants Phalacrocorax carbo and other large bird species as prey of goshawks Accipiter gentilis in De Wieden | journal = De Takkeling | volume = 16 | pages = 85–91 }}</ref><ref>Madsen, J. (2010). ''Waders: their breeding, haunts and watchers''. A&C Black.</ref> 
[[File:オオタカのヒナ.jpg|thumb|right|Juvenile in Japan with a young bird prey item]]

Corvids as aforementioned are quite important prey. Although they take fewer passerines than ''Accipiter''s do, smaller types of songbirds can still be regionally important to the diet. This is especially true of the [[thrush (bird)|thrush]]es which are often delivered to nests in Europe. 17 species of thrush have been identified in goshawk food across their range. The numerous {{convert|103|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Eurasian blackbird]] (''Turdus merula'') is often most reported from this family and can even be the main prey at some locations such as in the [[Netherlands]] (23.5% of prey by number) and in [[Norway]] (just over 14% by number and two studies showed thrushes collectively make up nearly half of the prey items in Norwegian nests).<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Verdal/><ref name= Schreven/><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Sonerud |first1= G. A. |last2= Steen |first2= R. |last3= Selås |first3= V. |last4= Aanonsen |first4= O. M. |last5= Aasen |first5= G. H. |last6= Fagerland |first6= K. L. |last7= Fossa |first7= A. |last8= Kristiansen |first8= L. |last9= Low |first9= L. M. |last10= Ronning |first10= M. E. |last11= Skouen |first11= S. K. |last12= Asakskogen |first12= E. |last13= Johansen |first13= H. M. |last14= Johnsen |first14= J. T. |last15= Karlsen |first15= L. T. |last16= Nyhus |first16= G. C. |last17= Roed |first17= L. T. |last18= Skar |first18= K. |last19= Sveen |first19= B.-A. |last20= Tvelten |first20= R. |last21= Slagsvold |first21= T. |year= 2014 |title= Evolution of parental roles in provisioning birds: diet determines role asymmetry in raptors |journal= Behavioral Ecology |volume= 25 |issue= 4|pages= 762–772 |doi=10.1093/beheco/aru053|doi-access= free }}</ref> All common ''[[Turdus]]'' species are taken in some numbers in Europe, being quite regular and conspicuous in the woodland edge zones most often patrolled by male goshawks, especially while singing in spring and summer. Even where larger, more nutritious prey is present such as at pheasant release sites, the abundant thrushes are more often delivered to the nest because of the ease of capture such as in [[Norway]].<ref name= Verdal/><ref name= Kenward2/> Thrush taken have ranged in size from some small birds to the {{convert|118|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[mistle thrush]] (''Turdus viscivorus''), Europe's largest thrush.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name=Drennan/><ref name= Penteriani/> Beyond corvids and thrushes, most passerines encountered by Eurasian goshawks are substantially smaller and are often ignored under most circumstances in favour of more sizable prey. Nonetheless, more than a hundred passerines have been recorded their diet beyond these families. Most widespread passerine families from Europe have occasional losses to goshawks, including [[lark]]s, [[swallow]]s, [[nuthatch]]es, [[treecreeper]]s, [[Eurasian wren|wrens]], [[Old World warbler|warbler]]s, [[Old World flycatcher|flycatcher]]s, [[pipit]]s and [[wagtail]]s, [[starling]]s, [[Emberizidae|buntings]], [[finch]]es and [[Passeridae|sparrow]]s. Avian prey has even ranged to as small as the {{convert|5.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[goldcrest]] (''Regulus regulus''), one of the smallest birds in Europe.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Zawadzka/> Among smaller types of passerines, one of the most widely reported are [[finch]]es and, in some widespread studies, somewhat substantial numbers of finches of many species may actually be taken. Finches tend to fly more conspicuously as they cover longer distances, often bounding or undulating as they do, over the canopy than most forest songbirds, which may make them more susceptible to goshawk attacks than other small songbirds.<ref name="Møller"/><ref name= Bezzel>{{cite journal |last1= Bezzel |first1= E. |last2= Rust |first2= R. |last3= Kechele |first3= W. |year= 1997 |title= Nahrungswahl südbayerischer Habichte Accipiter gentilis während der Brutzeit |journal= Ornithologischer Anzeiger |volume= 36 |pages= 19–30 }}</ref> Non-passerine upland birds taken by goshawks in small numbers include but are not limited to [[nightjar]]s, [[Swift (bird)|swift]]s, [[European bee-eater|bee-eater]]s, [[kingfisher]]s, [[European roller|roller]]s, [[hoopoe]]s and [[parrot]]s.<ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name="Mañosa"/><ref name= Abuladze /><ref name="Møller"/>

===Other mammals===
[[File:DSC43940 A.jpg|thumb|left|A goshawk preying on a [[brown rat]] in a fairly urban area of Moscow.]]
Outside of the squirrel family, relatively few other types of [[rodent]]s are taken in many regions. [[Cricetidae|Microtine rodents]] which are so essential to most northern non-accipitrine hawks and a majority of owls are at best a secondary contributor to goshawk diets, even though 26 species have been reported in their diet. Exceptionally, in a study of the [[Carpathian mountains]] of [[Ukraine]], the {{convert|27.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[common vole]] (''Microtus arvalis'') was the second most numerous prey species.<ref name= White>{{cite journal |last1= White |first1= C.
